title: Dog Bloody Discharge 4 Weeks After Heat: Is it an Emergency?
meta_desc: Bloody discharge 4 weeks after a heat cycle can indicate Pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection. Learn why you need immediate vet care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Bloody or pus-like discharge four to eight weeks after a dog's heat cycle is a primary symptom of Pyometra, a life-threatening uterine infection that requires immediate emergency veterinary intervention.

What does it mean if my dog has bloody discharge four weeks after her heat cycle?

If your dog is experiencing a bloody, brownish, or pus-like discharge from her vulva approximately four to eight weeks after her heat cycle has ended, it is a major red flag. This timing coincides with the diestrus phase of the reproductive cycle, where the hormone progesterone is at its highest. This hormonal state can cause the lining of the uterus to thicken and, in some cases, lead to a severe bacterial infection known as Pyometra.

Is bloody discharge after a dog's heat cycle a veterinary emergency?

  • Pyometra is a life-threatening emergency that requires immediate attention.
  • An infected uterus fills with bacteria and toxins that can enter the bloodstream, leading to sepsis, organ failure, and death.
  • Even if your dog is acting normal, an "open" pyometra (where discharge is visible) can turn into a "closed" pyometra, which is even more dangerous as the uterus may rupture.

How can a photo of the discharge help the veterinary triage process?

  • Clear photos of discharge on the vulva, floor, or bedding provide visual evidence of color and consistency.
  • Visuals help the veterinary team differentiate between lingering heat cycles and active infections.
  • Sharing a photo allows the clinic to prepare for your arrival and prioritize immediate diagnostics like bloodwork or an ultrasound.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

A bloody vaginal discharge 4-6 weeks after estrus in a dog, especially in older bitches (>5 years old), can be indicative of pyometra, a hormonally mediated diestrual disorder characterized by cystic endometrial hyperplasia with secondary bacterial infection. Pyometra can present with abnormal vaginal discharge and signs of systemic illness. Diagnostic differentials include subinvolution of placental sites (SIPS), metritis, vaginitis, and cystitis. If the animal is systemically ill, stabilization with IV fluids, supportive care, and antibiotic therapy based on culture and sensitivity testing of the vulvar discharge is warranted. Ovariohysterectomy is recommended after initial stabilization if the animal is extremely ill or if future reproduction is unimportant.

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1399)

faqs.question: Can my dog have Pyometra if she doesn't have a fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. Many dogs with Pyometra do not have an elevated temperature in the early stages. The presence of discharge after a heat cycle is a more reliable warning sign than a fever.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Common secondary symptoms include increased thirst (drinking more water than usual), frequent urination, lethargy, loss of appetite, and a bloated or painful abdomen.

faqs.question: Is Pyometra preventable?
faqs.answer: Yes, the only 100% effective way to prevent Pyometra is to have your dog spayed (ovariohysterectomy), as this involves the surgical removal of the uterus and ovaries.
